main_summary.summary: Dr. Robert B. Hayling (b. November 20, 1929, Tallahassee, Florida), the St. Augustine dentist who led the local Civil Rights movement in 1963-64, recounts his path from Florida A&M and Air Force service through Meharry Medical College and into the leadership of the St. Augustine campaign that helped force passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 14, 2011 for the Civil Rights History Project. Hayling describes Air Force toxicology work at Wright-Patterson, dental training at Meharry under Z. Alexander Looby, the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall after the Looby bombing, his 1960 arrival in St. Augustine to inherit Dr. Rudolph Gordon's practice, and his recruitment as advisor to the NAACP Youth Council after Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr. was forced to flee to Gainesville. Central episodes include the Quadricentennial exclusion of Black St. Augustinians, George Reedy's double-cross of Hayling's delegation before the Lyndon B. Johnson visit, the September 1963 Klan rally where Hayling and three others were beaten with ax handles and threatened with immolation, the break with Roy Wilkins and the NAACP, Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr.'s Yale spring-break tutorial volunteers, Mary Peabody's arrest at the Monson Motor Lodge, and Hayling's refusal to honor the LBJ-requested demonstration pause. Closes with his 1966 relocation to Cocoa, Florida and the June 20, 2003 naming of Dr. Hayling Place.
key_themes: SCLC and Yale Divinity School alliance
Civil Rights Act of 1964 passage pressure
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hayling is the principal eyewitness to the St. Augustine movement, the campaign whose escalation in spring and early summer 1964 created the political pressure that helped force Lyndon B. Johnson and the Senate to complete passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 on July 2, 1964. The interview supplies first-person testimony on three episodes that bear directly on the historical record: the federal-government double-cross orchestrated by George Reedy in advance of the Vice President's 1963 Quadricentennial visit; the September 1963 Klan rally at which Hayling, James Hauser, James Jackson, and Clyde Jenkins were beaten and threatened with being doused in gasoline and set on fire before the Klan's announced intent was interrupted by an Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who recorded the rally and called the Florida Highway Patrol; and Hayling's explicit refusal, when LBJ asked civil rights organizations for a two-week cessation of demonstrations, to comply on behalf of the St. Augustine chapter ("fool me once, shame on you; fool me twice, shame on me").

Hayling also documents the institutional rupture between the local St. Augustine movement and Roy Wilkins's national NAACP, in which the local chapter surrendered its charter rather than accept instructions from New York, and the subsequent alliance with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s SCLC and Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr.'s Yale Divinity School. The testimony names the network of Northern white financial supporters (Esther Burgess, Mrs. Rowe, Mrs. Campbell, Mary Peabody) and the local Black support circle (Henry Twine, Oscar Turner, Clyde Jenkins, Fannie Fulwood, Elizabeth Hawthorne, E.G. Chase, Mr. Pennington) who sustained the campaign when both the federal government and the national NAACP declined to back it.

chapters.title: Our Host in St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the September 14, 2011 session at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine, Florida, thanking local civil rights pioneer Dr. Robert B. Hayling, who recounts being born in Tallahassee on November 20, 1929, son of a Florida A&M tailoring instructor and a homemaker.

chapters.title: Every Child an Advanced Degree
chapters.summary: Hayling describes his Creole-descended mother from New Orleans and his father from Grenada who taught at Florida A&M for some three decades, his older brother Charles Jr. and sisters Frances and Yvonne, and a family in which all four children earned college and advanced degrees, three of them in education.

chapters.title: Stranger Than Hen's Teeth
chapters.summary: He recounts entering the Air Force in 1951, training as a medical and laboratory technician at Sheppard Air Force Base, earning his commission as a second lieutenant through Officer Candidate School in December 1952, and a coveted assignment in aeromedical toxicology research at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, where his fellow officers were captains, majors, and colonels.

chapters.title: Always Wanted to Be a Dentist
chapters.summary: Hayling tells how he turned down an early promotion to captain to take an early release for dental school, having always wanted to be a dentist or veterinarian, and how he and his new wife, a Miami schoolteacher he brought to Dayton, gave up the comforts of officer life and a beloved boxer puppy named Mandana as she returned to Miami before he started at Meharry in September 1955.

chapters.title: A Class of Tremendous Honors
chapters.summary: He describes the difficult adjustment to study among accomplished peers at Meharry Medical College in Nashville, naming classmate Fred Fielder who later rose to be dean of the dental school, and a cohort whose members went on to honors in dentistry, entrepreneurship, and financial consulting.

chapters.title: Looby's House Was Dynamited
chapters.summary: Hayling recalls his dental-law instructor Z. Alexander Looby, lead attorney for school desegregation, having his home dynamited in 1960, blowing out windows of Hayling's nearby Meharry dormitory, the movement leaders he came to know (John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, James Bevel), and slipping out to join the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall protesting the bombing.

chapters.title: C.K. Steele and the Bus Boycott
chapters.summary: Hayling recounts the two Tallahassee movements he knew on visits home: Reverend C.K. Steele's bus boycott that bankrupted the transit system and earned him a monument at the bus station, and the work of Patricia and Priscilla Stephens and attorney John Due, who were jailed in a wire-fenced setup with no privacy and later wrote the book Freedom in the Family.

chapters.title: The Thousand-Dollar Supplement
chapters.topic: How Florida Paid for Dental School
chapters.summary: He explains that Florida, lacking a dental school for Black students, paid Meharry a thousand-dollar yearly supplement for each Black Floridian enrolled, which along with his service record and the GI Bill financed his education and spared him the heavy student debt of later generations.

chapters.title: Sky Cap at Newark Airport
chapters.summary: Hayling describes financing dental school by working New York summers, as an overnight sky cap at Newark Airport for the Port Authority and as a waiter at a famous Sheepshead Bay seafood restaurant near Coney Island that employed a hundred or more waiters and owned its own shrimp boats, staying with his father's sister and other relatives in Brooklyn.

chapters.title: Driving the Coast for an Office
chapters.summary: He recounts taking the Florida dental board exam over the July 4 weekend of 1960 and driving the state from the east coast to Key West and up to Pensacola scouting cities with Black dentists, settling on St. Augustine, where the late Dr. Gordon's office had become available after a fatal car accident, over a Panama City option in the isolated panhandle.

chapters.title: They Rolled Up the Sidewalks
chapters.summary: Hayling tells how Dr. Gordon's widow welcomed him and introduced him to her late husband's largely white patient base, how the office still reeked of the expensive Cuban cigars Gordon smoked by the carton, and how his practice boomed at a December 1960 housewarming as patients who had gone two years without care returned, even as he drove to Jacksonville nightspots to escape a town that drew its curtains at sundown.

chapters.title: The Jail Dentist
chapters.summary: He describes becoming the city, county, and state-prison-farm jail dentist of St. Johns County and St. Augustine, scheduling jail patients apart so armed guards would not unsettle others, and treating many deputies and even Sheriff L.O. Davis on the house under no formal arrangement, insisting he was just a good fellow until, in his words, he became radioactive once he got involved with civil rights.

chapters.title: A Delegation Came to My Office
chapters.summary: Hayling explains he was never formally sent by the NAACP, and recounts how a youth protest movement begun under Reverend Thomas A. Wright lost its advisor when Wright's wife was fired and he was pressured out of St. Augustine to Gainesville, after which a delegation of youngsters asked Hayling, around 1963, to advise their picketing and demonstrations.

chapters.title: A Hamburger and a Coke
chapters.summary: He describes the youth following the rules of the sidewalk while merchants merely tolerated them, the lunch-counter request the group devised for the news media (ordering a hamburger and a coke), and the local NAACP women who supported them: chapter president Fannie Fulwood and secretary Elizabeth Hawthorne, who rode her bicycle across town despite a spinal deformity to serve as the youth council's stabilizing secretary.

chapters.title: Not One Black Appointed
chapters.summary: Hayling recounts regional NAACP director Ruby Hurley warning that the organization was not ready for a major St. Augustine campaign, just as the city prepared to spend a federal $400,000 appropriation on its 400th-anniversary celebration without appointing a single Black person to any committee, despite Black principals, professors, and a school superintendent, Mrs. Reddick, and college president Dr. Puryear.

chapters.title: A Letter to the Vice President
chapters.summary: He describes the grand anniversary dinner at the Ponce de Leon Hotel in late spring 1963, drawing the ambassador from Spain, the vice president, senators, and the governor, and how the group wrote Vice President Lyndon Johnson asking him not to attend a segregated function, generating enough heat that Johnson sent aide George Reedy ahead to meet repeatedly with Hayling.

chapters.title: A Secretary and a Tape Recorder
chapters.summary: Hayling recounts being seated in an alcove overlooking the Grand Ballroom, dining only alongside Secret Service and FBI agents, and the promised next-morning meeting with the City Commission that turned out to be only the mayor's secretary and her tape recorder, the group's request for a biracial committee taped and never answered, and the stunt of dressing the plainclothes Lincolnville officer in a uniform downtown to claim the city already had a Black policeman.

chapters.title: Back to the Think Tanks
chapters.summary: Hayling admits the group had no battle plan and had to regroup after each defeat made them look foolish, while the national and regional NAACP warned they were generating more heat than it could handle, and credits a restaurant across from his office run by Mr. Pennington, who fed any demonstrator connected to the movement and was reimbursed at day's end.

chapters.title: Cut Off at the Knees
chapters.summary: He thanks the Elks, who lent the second floor of the Elks Rest for meetings as the press gathered, describes the dual-headed structure in which the youth stayed out of negotiations while adult spokesmen like Henry Twine, Oscar Turner, and Clyde Jenkins led, the formal demands to let Black residents sit for the fireman's and policeman's exams, and the establishment ordering the college's faculty and 300 to 400 students not to participate on pain of losing their jobs.

chapters.title: Tobias Simon, ACLU Fireball
chapters.summary: He recounts threatening the college with a lawsuit when instructors denied makeup work to student demonstrators, and acquiring the services of Miami ACLU attorney Tobias Simon, a civil rights fireball lost too soon to a heart attack after running a Boston Marathon, before turning to a prominent local student then at a North Carolina college, Hank Thomas.

chapters.title: Sammy Davis Put Up the Bond
chapters.summary: Hayling tells how the Freedom Rider Hank Thomas, who later owned McDonald's franchises and Marriott hotels in Atlanta, staged a one-man drugstore sit-in in St. Augustine and was jailed on a hundred-dollar bond that the NAACP let it be known Sammy Davis Jr. had paid, then was ordered out of town, but the arrest had started the movement and spread word through the community.

chapters.title: The Meeting Was Canceled
chapters.summary: He recounts becoming the first African American admitted with full privileges to the local and state dental association, and how, when his rotating position made him due for election to leadership, pressure on the other dentists got the meeting abruptly canceled, with members afterward phoning to ask why he had missed gatherings his secretary was never told about.

chapters.title: Connie Lynch Comes to Town
chapters.summary: Hayling describes the local Klan, calling itself the Klavern, papering downtown with handbills for a major September 1963 rally on a field along US 1, bringing in out-of-state leaders Connie Lynch of California and J.B. Stoner of Atlanta, and how he and three others drove out that night to park on the roadside and watch the rally from across the highway.

chapters.title: Piled Like Cordwood
chapters.summary: He recounts being spotted with James Jackson, James Hauser, and Clyde Jenkins, fleeing down a side road only to meet a moat the Klan had dug across it, being dragged from the car, robbed, and exposed as an NAACP member, then beaten with ax handles and baseball bats and piled semiconscious like cordwood on the rally stage.

chapters.title: Smelling a Burning
chapters.summary: Hayling describes an Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who infiltrated the rally with a recorder, then phoned the Tallahassee highway patrol, and the announcer asking the crowd whether they had smelled a man burn and threatening to douse the captives with gasoline, before law enforcement arrived and the men were taken first to Flagler Hospital's emergency room, then by mortician E.G. Chase's hearse to the Black Brewster Hospital in Jacksonville.

chapters.title: Damage to a Dentist's Arm
chapters.summary: He summarizes injuries he would take to his grave, losing eleven teeth and suffering deliberate damage to the right arm of a right-handed dentist that he had to rehabilitate to keep practicing, and reflects that four years of honorable Air Force service did not win him a white citizen's rights, that L.O. Davis later branded him a communist, and that the local Klan leader was Holstead Hoss Manucy, a convicted moonshiner deputized with many Klansmen as auxiliary officers by the sheriff he was close to.

chapters.part: Breaking Away to King and the Northern Allies
chapters.title: The Mailman Will Bring Your Charter
chapters.summary: He recounts repeated calls from Roy Wilkins's New York office instructing the movement how to proceed, telling Wilkins to send anyone who could lead St. Augustine better, and when Wilkins threatened to withdraw the charter, replying that the mailman would bring it back, mailing the charter to New York by special delivery to resign before the NAACP could fire them.

chapters.part: Breaking Away to King and the Northern Allies
chapters.title: Up the Creek Without a Paddle
chapters.topic: Seeking SCLC and Refusing to Stop
chapters.summary: Hayling describes weekend trips to Atlanta after the break, meeting Julian Bond, the SNCC staff, and Ella Baker, and people connected to Daddy King and Ebenezer Baptist Church, with C.K. Steele on the SCLC board helping make contacts, and how, when President Johnson asked civil rights groups for a two-week halt to demonstrations, the twice-double-crossed St. Augustine chapter refused to stop until the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was signed, with King in St. Augustine on June 30, 1964.

chapters.part: Breaking Away to King and the Northern Allies
chapters.title: Yale Students for Spring Break
chapters.summary: Hayling recounts Reverend William Sloane Coffin Jr. of Yale Divinity School arranging for Yale students to spend their spring break in St. Augustine running a tutorial and fix-up program rather than going to the beaches, a publicity coup funded by the students and their families, the white volunteers housed in Black families' homes, and a fleeting chance to speak with Dr. King when a St. Augustine group traveled to a King rally in Tallahassee.

chapters.title: A Play About St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Hayling recounts weekend trips to New York and Boston, visits to Harvard and MIT, the Yale students staging a drama about St. Augustine with characters playing L.O. Davis and Judge Mathis, and the wealthy supporters who funded the movement, including Esther Burgess, wife of the first Black Episcopal bishop John Burgess, and Mary Peabody, the seventy-two-year-old mother of the sitting Massachusetts governor, arrested alongside Hayling at the Monson Motor Lodge.

chapters.title: Acid in the Swimming Pool
chapters.summary: Asked to reflect on the campaign's culmination in the June 1964 signing of the Civil Rights Act, Hayling recalls Monson Motor Lodge owner James Brock pouring acid into the pool as demonstrators swam, a picture that circled the world and even ran on a Russian front page, and a man who died an uncompromising segregationist though his daughter came to differ.

chapters.title: Lyndon Johnson Caught Us Again
chapters.summary: Hayling explains that after fulfilling his five-year obligation to Florida by practicing in St. Augustine, he opened a booming practice in Cocoa near Cape Kennedy with help from Black mortician Rudy Stone and physician Dr. Jerkins, only for Johnson to move the space center's control center and payroll to the Johnson Space Center, and why he chose Cocoa's younger NASA-technician community over inheriting a Tallahassee practice from his family dentist Dr. Campbell.

chapters.title: The Award of Valor
chapters.summary: Hayling describes receiving the city of St. Augustine's Award of Valor, presented by the mayor and commissioners at ACCORD where Dorothy Cotton of the SCLC spoke, and honoring the family of Reverend Goldie Eubanks with a Waterford crystal eagle-in-flight award, attended by his Yale- and Stanford-educated daughter, her Princeton- and Harvard-trained husband Chris, and their two grandsons from Singapore.

chapters.title: While I Can Still Smell the Roses
chapters.topic: A Street and the Foot Soldiers
chapters.summary: He recalls the June 20, 2003 renaming of Scott Street to Dr. Hayling Place, attended by twenty-one Haylings and his infant grandson Roman, Commissioner Earl Jones's wish to honor him while he could still smell the roses, praise for Jones's mother Maddie Jones, who worked at his office for years when others shunned him as a leper for fear it would be shot up or dynamited, and the street sign he carries to gatherings of the movement's foot soldiers as the interview closes.

loc_video.caption: Robert Bagner Hayling oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 14
